section: Effect of annealing temperature on the microstructure and tensile properties of Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel

context_update.summary: Current chunk encompasses equilibrium phase diagram calculation (Fig. 2) and microstructure evolution of hot-rolled and annealed specimens (700–1200°C). It describes: Thermo-Calc predicts ferrite forms first, austenite by peritectic reaction, and κ-carbide appears below 823°C via γ→α+κ; δ-ferrite always present. Hot-rolled specimen shows banded structure, coarse ferrite, and α/γ phase areas; κ-carbide at boundaries; EDS confirms Al-rich α, Mn/C-rich γ, and Mn/Al-rich κ. XRD reveals κ peaks at 700, 800, 850°C, no γ at 700°C; lattice parameters α 2.89–2.91 Å, γ 3.65–3.67 Å, κ 3.76–3.79 Å. OM and SEM demonstrate κ-carbide dissolution between 850–900°C; at 700°C, lamellar κ with N-W orientation; at 800°C, globular κ without orientation. Grain sizes: α2 and γ grow rapidly above 900°C, α1 above 1000°C. Boundary κ-carbide along α/α boundaries at 900–1200°C, formed during quenching, some with N-W orientation. EDS line scans show element partitioning. XRD volume fractions: γ ~60% at 900°C, range 57–63% from 900–1200°C; no ordering detected. Key figures: 2–14.
processing_route: annealing at 700–1200°C for 1 h, water quenching
microstructure: α1 and α2 grain size evolution
mechanisms: N-W orientation relationship between κ and α

context_update.summary: This chunk finalizes the microstructure evolution section with XRD-determined phase volume fractions (Fig. 14) and covers the full tensile properties and fracture analysis. XRD confirms that κ-carbide dissolves between 850–900 °C, exceeding the Thermo-Calc prediction of 823 °C. No γ is present at 700 °C and no κ at 900 °C. The γ fraction increases to ~60% at 900 °C and then stays within 57–63% up to 1200 °C, peaking around 1000 °C. Boundary κ is undetectable by XRD. TEM reveals no superlattice ordering after 1 h annealing. Tensile testing (Fig. 15, Table 1) shows continuous yielding except at 700 °C; higher annealing temperature lowers strength, while elongation peaks at 900 °C (36.6%), giving the best strength-ductility combination (UTS 855 MPa, YS 651 MPa, PSE 31 GPa%). Work hardening behavior differs between κ-containing and κ-free conditions. No TRIP/TWIP is observed. Fractography (Fig. 17) links large secondary cracks to banded microstructure and identifies mixed ductile (γ) and quasi-cleavage (α) fracture, with κ promoting cleavage at 800 °C; the ductile area is more pronounced at 900 °C than 1000 °C, and the role of boundary κ could not be resolved.

elongation increases up to 900 °C then decreases
work hardening rate reverses with temperature in κ-containing specimens when ε < 5%
κ-free specimens show lower initial work hardening rate
high initial hardening rate drops rapidly in κ-containing specimens

boundary κ effect on fracture unresolved

Summary: EDS line scans show element partitioning. XRD volume fractions: γ ~60% at 900°C, range 57–63% from 900–1200°C; no ordering detected. Key figures: 2–14. This chunk finalizes the microstructure evolution section with XRD-determined phase volume fractions (Fig. 14) and covers the full tensile properties and fracture analysis. XRD confirms that κ-carbide dissolves between 850–900 °C, exceeding the Thermo-Calc prediction of 823 °C. No γ is present at 700 °C and no κ at 900 °C. The γ fraction increases to ~60% at 900 °C and then stays within 57–63% up to 1200 °C, peaking around 1000 °C. Boundary κ is undetectable by XRD. TEM reveals no superlattice ordering after 1 h annealing. Tensile testing (Fig. 15, Table 1) shows continuous yielding except at 700 °C; higher annealing temperature lowers strength, while elongation peaks at 900 °C (36.6%), giving the best strength-ductility combination (UTS 855 MPa, YS 651 MPa, PSE 31 GPa%). Work hardening behavior differs between κ-containing and κ-free conditions. No TRIP/TWIP is observed. Fractography (Fig. 17) links large secondary cracks to banded microstructure and identifies mixed ductile (γ) and quasi-cleavage (α) fracture, with κ promoting cleavage at 800 °C; the ductile area is more pronounced at 900 °C than 1000 °C, and the role of boundary κ could not be resolved.

context_update.summary: This chunk covers the latter part of fracture surface analysis and begins the Discussion (4, 4.1). It details microstructure evolution during annealing: eutectoid decomposition γ→α+κ at 700°C, N-W orientation relationship destruction at 800°C, κ morphology change to globular, elemental partitioning (Mn, Al in κ highest), dissolution sequence (α/γ boundary κ dissolves first, α/α boundary κ last), actual κ dissolution temperature (850–900°C vs. 823°C Thermo-Calc), and boundary κ formation during quenching at ≥900°C with needle-like FCC particles in α at 1200°C. It provides quantitative grain size data for α1, γ, α2 from 900 to 1200°C (Fig. 9) and XRD volume fractions from HR to 1200°C (Fig. 14), showing a slight decrease of α1 at 950°C due to phase boundary movement. The banded structure persists. Subsequent Discussion will likely relate microstructure to tensile properties and work hardening.
microstructure: κ along α/α and α/γ boundaries
needle-like/plate-like FCC particles in α at 1200°C
mechanisms: N-W orientation relationship destruction with κ growth and temperature increase
elemental competition: Mn,C between α-κ; Al between γ-κ
higher dissolution temperature of α/α boundary κ due to Al
phase boundary movement causing α1 grain size decrease at 950°C
grain size controlled by recovery/recrystallization and phase transformation

context_update.summary: This chunk concludes the microstructure evolution discussion (end of 4.1) and presents the full correlation between tensile properties and microstructure (section 4.2) along with the conclusions (section 5). It reports that α1 grain size decreases when annealing temperature rises from 900 to 950 °C due to α/γ boundary movement. The mechanical properties of all annealing temperatures are given in Table 1: UTS, YS, UE, and PSE. UTS and YS decrease monotonically with temperature, while UE and PSE peak at 900 °C (36.6%, 31 GPa%). Yield phenomenon occurs only at 700 °C. Microstructure-property links are established: below 900 °C, ductility is controlled by γ phase fraction and κ-carbide promotes microcracks; above 900 °C, grain size becomes the main controlling factor. Fractographs (Fig. 17) show mixed ductile (γ) and quasi-cleavage (α) fracture; the ductile area diminishes at 1000 °C. Needle-like particles in α at 1200 °C promote cracking. The conclusions summarize κ dissolution (850–900 °C), maximum γ fraction (~63% at 1000 °C), boundary κ existence over 900 °C, and rapid grain growth above 900 °C. A comparison with other Fe-Mn-Al-C steels (Table 2) attributes the lower elongation to high α fraction and absence of intragranular κ, and suggests increasing C to offset strength loss from low Mn. This chunk provides all quantitative property data, key SEM evidence, and the overarching processing-structure-property framework needed for interpreting the steel's behavior.
processing_route: annealing at 700–1200 °C for 1 h, water quenching
properties: 900 °C optimum: UTS 855, YS 651, UE 36.6%, PSE 31 GPa%
mechanisms: Ductility below 900 °C controlled by γ fraction
κ-carbide strengthens but microcracks initiate preferentially
Work hardening: high initial rate drops quickly due to microcracking near κ
